title: Propelling catalytic structures using active phase separation
abstract: Living systems routinely consume energy to achieve motility, often using intricate biomolecular machinery. In this work, we show that active droplets can sustain indefinite self-propulsion of a spherical colloid in an otherwise homogeneous, isotropic, and autonomous environment. Our proposed minimal mechanism consists of phase-separating proteins, enzymes passivating them, and complementary enzymes anchored to the colloid surface that reactivate the proteins. This passivation-activation cycle gives rise to a symmetry breaking - nucleation and stabilization of a condensate near the colloid surface, which in turn exerts a repulsive force on the colloid. We numerically demonstrate that this mechanism can propel micron-sized colloids at speeds of up to a hundred microns per second. This propulsion mode is strongly resistant to Brownian fluctuations and external forces, suggesting that propulsion mechanisms based on biomolecular condensates may offer a complementary, motor-free route to biological transport.
summary: The paper proposes a mechanism where active phase separation, driven by enzymatic cycles, can propel particles through a medium. This provides a way for biological transport to occur via biomolecular condensates without relying on traditional molecular motors.
main_problem: Investigating how active phase separation can be used to achieve self-propulsion of a colloid without the need for complex molecular motors.
main_result: Demonstrated that a passivation-activation cycle of phase-separating proteins can induce symmetry breaking and create a repulsive force that propels colloids at speeds up to 100 microns per second.
method: Numerical simulations of an active droplet system involving phase-separating proteins and enzymatic reactions.
model_or_system: A spherical colloid in a homogeneous environment containing phase-separating proteins, enzymes that passivate the proteins, and complementary enzymes anchored to the colloid surface that reactivate them.
key_observables: Colloid propulsion speed, stability of the condensate, and resistance to Brownian fluctuations.
important_parameters: Micron-sized scale, reaction rates of the passivation-activation cycle, and presence of external forces/Brownian noise.
main_assumptions: The system is autonomous, homogeneous, and isotropic prior to the symmetry-breaking event.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the biological motivation, proposes a minimal mechanism based on enzymatic cycles, presents numerical evidence for propulsion and speed, and discusses the robustness of this motor-free transport mode.
